items.q: In Pavlov's experiment, what is the conditioned response?
items.correct: Salivation to the bell alone, after pairing with food.
items.misconception: Salivation to the food (which is automatic).
items.random_wrong: The dog ringing the bell itself.
items.why: Students confuse the unconditioned response (automatic salivation to food) with the conditioned response (learned salivation to bell). The conditioned response is the LEARNED one.

items.correct: Removing an unpleasant stimulus to INCREASE a behaviour.
items.misconception: Punishing a behaviour to decrease it.
items.random_wrong: Giving a reward to decrease behaviour.
items.why: Students confuse negative reinforcement with punishment. Both involve something unpleasant, but reinforcement (positive or negative) always INCREASES behaviour; punishment decreases it.

items.q: What are the three elements of Beck's negative triad?
items.correct: Negative views of self, world, and future.
items.misconception: Negative views of past, present, and future.
items.why: Students often substitute 'past' for 'self' or 'world'. The triad is about the DOMAINS of negative thought (self/world/future), not time periods.
It's Avolition. “Auditory hallucinations” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: DSM-5 requires schizophrenia symptoms to persist for at least how long?
items.why: Correct answer: 1 month. “1 week” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately what percentage of schizophrenia hallucinations are auditory (Slade & Bentall)?
items.why: Exact answer: 70. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
